questions.question: What is scintillation in satellite optical communication?

options.text: The color of the beam changing
The satellite moving across the sky

questions.explanation: Scintillation refers to intensity fluctuations (twinkling) caused by atmospheric turbulence. Refractive index variations along the beam path create focusing and defocusing effects that change the received intensity.

questions.question: What does the Fried parameter (r₀) measure?
questions.explanation: The Fried parameter (r₀) is the atmospheric coherence length—the aperture diameter over which wavefront distortions are less than one radian. Larger r₀ means better seeing conditions. Typical values are 5-20 cm at visible wavelengths.

questions.question: Why does atmospheric loss increase at higher zenith angles?

options.text: The light passes through more atmosphere

questions.explanation: At higher zenith angles, light travels through a longer atmospheric path (airmass = sec(zenith_angle)). More atmosphere means more absorption, scattering, and turbulence, increasing total loss.

questions.question: What is the purpose of adaptive optics in ground stations?

options.text: To correct wavefront distortions from turbulence

questions.explanation: Adaptive optics uses deformable mirrors to correct wavefront distortions caused by atmospheric turbulence in real-time. This can improve the Strehl ratio by 3-10x and significantly improve fiber coupling efficiency.

questions.question: How does aperture averaging help mitigate scintillation?

options.text: Large apertures average over many turbulent cells

questions.explanation: A large aperture collects light from many independent turbulent cells simultaneously. The intensity fluctuations from different cells partially cancel out, reducing the overall scintillation variance.

questions.question: Why is 1550 nm often preferred over 850 nm for satellite links?

options.text: Better atmospheric transmission and less turbulence effect

questions.explanation: 1550 nm has higher atmospheric transmission (~92% vs ~88% at zenith) and turbulence effects scale as λ^-1/5, so longer wavelengths are less affected. Additionally, 1550 nm is compatible with telecom fiber networks.

questions.question: Daytime satellite QKD is impossible due to atmospheric effects.
questions.explanation: False. While daytime operation is much more challenging due to high background light (sunlight), it is possible with narrow spectral filtering, tight spatial filtering, and short temporal gating. Several groups are working on daytime satellite QKD.
